steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Build a React single-page Hashi (Bridges) puzzle game with a retro terminal look: black background, green monospace text, no CSS frameworks. Source goes under /app/src/; the test harness serves the production build at /app/dist on http://localhost:3000, so `npm run build` should succeed and you don't need to keep a dev server running.

clicking island A then island B cycles the bridge between them through none → single → double → none. Right-click or Ctrl+click on an existing bridge removes it entirely (single or double, both go to none) — the click target is the bridge `<line>` element itself (anywhere along the line, including its midpoint), not the island endpoints; attach the `onContextMenu` and Ctrl+click handlers to each rendered `<line>`, and call `e.preventDefault()` on the contextmenu event so the browser menu doesn't swallow it. For a double bridge that means both parallel `<line>`s individually carry the handlers (a single handler on a wrapping `<g>` is not sufficient — the click must register when targeted directly on either line), and removing either one clears the pair. Arrow keys move a visible cursor between islands; Enter selects an island, arrow keys move toward another, Enter toggles the bridge; Esc cancels. The cursor is parked on the first island (id 0) from page load and after any puzzle change, and the key listeners live on window/document — not on a focused DOM node — so a fresh page that has just been blurred still reacts to Enter without needing a prior arrow press. Invalid moves render a descriptive error inside an element with class error-message, cleared on the next valid move — and "cleared on the next valid move" means: when the second click of a pair produces an error, also reset the in-progress selection in the same state update, so the very next click starts a fresh A→B pair that runs the toggle (do not let the post-error click be interpreted as a deselect). The error-message element must be removed (or hidden) the moment a subsequent valid bridge toggle executes. The error text must be one of: "Cannot connect non-adjacent islands", "Max 2 bridges per pair", "Island would exceed its number", "Bridges would cross".

The board is a single SVG of 800×600 anchored to the viewport top-left — no centering, no page padding. Each grid unit is 40px, so an island at grid (x, y) renders at pixel (x*40, y*40). Islands are SVG circle elements; bridges are line elements (two parallel lines for a double). The keyboard cursor must be clearly visible but must not be a circle — use rect, stroke filter, group transform, anything else — so the board's circle count always equals the island count.

Islands are numbered circles with values 1–8; the number is the exact bridge count required at that island. Bridges are straight horizontal or vertical lines between two islands, max two per pair, never crossing. The puzzle is solved when every island's count is satisfied and all islands form one connected component.

Puzzle data lives at /app/src/data/puzzles.js with three puzzles, selectable via a single <select> dropdown whose options read "Easy", "Medium", and "Hard". Each puzzle exposes an `islands` array of `{ id, x, y, value }` where id is a 0-indexed integer unique within the puzzle, x and y are in grid units, and value is the required bridge count. Easy is the four-island ring at grid (2,2), (6,2), (2,6), (6,6) with every value=2; Medium has exactly five islands; Hard can be denser but must still fit the board. A Reset button clears all bridges on the current puzzle while leaving the islands in place. When the puzzle is solved, render a banner with class victory-banner containing the text SOLVED!.

One thing worth flagging from prototyping: the error-message and victory-banner only need to exist while their condition is active, and the dropdown handler should fully reset bridges when the puzzle changes — clearing the bridges map (and any selection/error/victory state) in the same state update that swaps the puzzle, so the next render shows zero `<line>` elements. The first mouse click on an island is just selection — the toggle runs on the second click against a different island, and once that toggle fires the selection clears so the next click starts a fresh pair. Resist the urge to layer invisible or wider `<line>` elements over the visible bridge for easier click detection: the total `<line>` count is observable and should equal the visible bridge count (one per single, two per double).

Game logic lives at /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js. Bridges are stored as Map<string, number> keyed by the canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" string where the endpoint with the smaller (x, then y) sorts first; coordinates in the key are grid units. canAddBridge(islands, bridges, from, to, delta) returning { valid, error } where error, when non-null, is one of the four strings above; computeCurrentCounts(islands, bridges) returning Map<islandId, number> keyed by the island's numeric id (handle id=0 carefully); checkConnectivity(islands, bridges) returning whether every island is reachable from every other; isVictory(islands, bridges) returning whether every island's count equals its value, no bridges cross, and the graph is connected.

canAddBridge should also guard the degenerate from === to case (valid: false; the error string there is unconstrained).



Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@8d3b79672224:/app#

Analysis: Starting fresh with an empty /app directory. I need to build a complete React Hashi puzzle game with specific requirements including retro terminal styling, SVG-based board, keyboard navigation, mouse interaction, and game logic.
Plan: First, I'll check what's already in /app, then set up the project structure with package.json, webpack config, and all source files.

steps.message:
Analysis: There's already a Vite + React project set up with dependencies installed. I need to check the existing source structure and then build out the Hashi game.
Plan: Let me check the existing source files, vite config, and index.html to understand the current structure before building the game.

Analysis: The project has a Vite + React setup with basic scaffolding. I need to build the complete Hashi game. Let me check the remaining existing files first.
Plan: Check existing files, then create the complete game: puzzles data, game logic utilities, and the main App component with all required features.

Analysis: I have a clear picture of the project structure. It's a Vite + React project with basic scaffolding. I need to create the complete Hashi game with:
1. Updated puzzles data (need to verify solvability)
2. Game logic in utils/hashiLogic.js
3. Full App component with SVG board, keyboard navigation, mouse interaction
4. Proper vite config for build output to /app/dist
Plan: I'll create all the necessary files:
1. Update vite.config.js to output to dist/
2. Update /app/src/data/puzzles.js with solvable puzzles
3. Create /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js with all game logic
4. Update /app/src/App.jsx with the complete game
5. Update /app/src/index.css with retro terminal styling
6. Run npm run build to verify

Analysis: The build succeeded! However, looking at the terminal output, the heredoc for hashiLogic.js appears to have been garbled during output (the terminal showed some content out of order). I need to verify the file was written correctly.
Plan: Let me verify the hashiLogic.js file content is correct, and if not, rewrite it properly.

Analysis: The build succeeded, the hashiLogic.js module exports all 5 required functions. The project builds cleanly. Let me verify the dist output exists properly and do a quick check on the App.jsx to make sure everything is in order.
Plan: Verify the dist directory has proper output, check that the App.jsx file is complete and correct, then verify the overall build works end-to-end.

steps.message:
Analysis: The build succeeded. All files are in place:
- /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js with 5 exported functions (getBridgeKey, canAddBridge, computeCurrentCounts, checkConnectivity, isVictory)
- /app/src/data/puzzles.js with 3 solvable puzzles (Easy=4 islands, Medium=5 islands, Hard=6 islands)
- /app/src/App.jsx with complete game implementation
- /app/src/index.css with retro terminal styling
- /app/dist/ has the built output

All requirements are met:
- SVG 800x600, grid 40px, islands as circles, bridges as lines
- Click A then B cycles: none→single→double→none
- Right-click/Ctrl+click on bridge line removes it (handlers on each <line>)
- Keyboard navigation with arrow keys, Enter, Esc on window
- Cursor is a rect (not circle)
- Error messages with correct strings, cleared on valid move, selection reset on error
- Victory banner with class victory-banner showing SOLVED!
- Puzzle selector and Reset button
- No CSS frameworks, retro terminal look
Plan: The task is complete. The build succeeds and all requirements are satisfied. Let me run a final verification to make sure everything is consistent.

Analysis: All verifications pass. The build succeeds, all required files are in place with correct content:
- hashiLogic.js exports all 5 required functions with all 4 error messages + degenerate case guard
- App.jsx has all required features: error-message class, victory-banner class, SOLVED! text, onContextMenu handlers on individual <line> elements, preventDefault, rect cursor (not circle), window event listeners, Escape handling
- Puzzles are correctly defined (Easy=4 islands ring, Medium=5 islands, Hard=6 islands), all solvable
- Build output is in /app/dist with proper index.html
- Retro terminal styling with no CSS frameworks
Plan: The task is complete. All requirements have been implemented and verified.
